text: The surgeon uses an optical access trocar to enter the abdomen infraumbilically, introducing a ten-millimeter, thirty-degree laparoscope. Three operative ports are placed under direct visualization: two five-millimeter ports in the right and left lateral abdomen, and a twelve-millimeter port in the left mid-abdomen. Graspers are used to reflect the transverse colon cephalad, exposing the ligament of Treitz.

The surgeon observes the patient's thin mesentery and identifies the superior mesenteric artery by its prominent pulsations. The ligament of Treitz is identified, and the duodenum is traced proximally. Using a harmonic scalpel, the peritoneum overlying the duodenum is lifted and opened. Graspers are employed to bluntly dissect the duodenum from its retroperitoneal attachments. The first and second portions of the duodenum show moderate dilation and a thin duodenal wall. The duodenum is carefully mobilized to facilitate a stapled anastomosis and prevent injury to nearby structures, such as the head of the pancreas.

The surgeon locates the ligament of Treitz and measures a forty-centimeter limb of proximal jejunum. The tension on the jejunal limb is assessed by bringing it to the mobilized duodenum. The duodenum is retracted, and a zero silk suture is used to approximate the anti-mesenteric borders of the duodenal and jejunal limbs.
The surgeon places a suture inferiorly, using it as a handle to retract downward, allowing for the placement of a superior approximation stitch between the duodenal and jejunal limbs.

The surgeon uses the working jaw of the harmonic scalpel to create and widen a jejunal enterotomy, followed by a duodenotomy. Bile promptly effluxes from the partially obstructed duodenum, indicating the release of obstruction.
The surgeon inserts a 45mm linear stapling device with a 2.5mm vascular staple load to the 30mm mark, creating a side-to-side duodenal jejunostomy between the duodenum and jejunum.

The surgeon closes the common opening of the side-to-side anastomosis with a single layer of full-thickness bites using zero silk suture, opting for a sutured closure to avoid narrowing the anastomosis.

The surgeon places multiple interrupted figure-of-eight stitches along the suture line and inspects it for defects. A bowel clamp is positioned ten centimeters distal to the anastomosis, and a bubble leak test is conducted by submerging the anastomosis in saline. The staple line is visually checked for bleeding, and an endoscope is passed beyond the anastomosis to assess its patency.

An O silk stitch is placed between the peritoneal lining of the mesentery and the anti-mesenteric border of the proximal jejunal limb to prevent rotation and relieve tension on the anastomosis. A number ten flat JP drain is positioned adjacent to the anastomosis.
